text: Good afternoon esteemed audience. We will be presenting a video on laparoscopic completion adrenalectomy technical nuances. Laparoscopic adrenalectomy is commonly performed nowadays for all its minimally invasive benefits. With advanced learning curves and better understanding of anatomy, it is possible to perform laparoscopic completion removal of a left over right adrenal gland.

A forty-year-old male had undergone a complete left and partial right adrenal gland removal for adrenal hyperplasia elsewhere by a chevron incision. Post-operatively he had abdominal wound infections and incision drainages over three months to form multiple abdominal incisional hernias. He presented with sequelae of adrenal hyperplasia after nine years and was advised completion of adrenal lectve with incisional hernia repair. MRI shows residual right adrenal gland.

The surgeon arranges the laparoscopic ports in a triangular configuration, with the adrenal gland positioned at the apex. A 5mm retractor port is placed laterally, and another 5mm port is positioned in the epigastrium for retraction. Severe adhesions are encountered near the right 12mm working port, which are dissected using a harmonic scalpel held in the left hand, while the telescope is introduced through the 5mm retractor port.
The surgeon incises the white line of Toldt and releases liver adhesions from previous adrenal gland surgery. A liver retractor is placed to enhance exposure, facilitating the dissection process.
The surgeon deflects the colon to reveal the renal vein, duodenum, and inferior vena cava (IVC), providing clear anatomical landmarks for safe dissection amidst severe adhesions.

The surgeon lifts the ureteric pedicle and identifies the plane with the duodenum, gently cauterizing the duodenum. The renal vein is exposed, and its superior margin is dilated. Girotas fascia is entered and lifted off the superior pole of the kidney. Due to fixed tissues from prior surgery, the peritoneum is incised, allowing the superior pole of the kidney to be deflected inferiorly.

The surgeon encounters calcification on the anterior surface of the residual adrenal gland, located lateral to the inferior vena cava (IVC). Amidst severe adhesions, blunt dissection with suction is performed to identify and separate the lateral margin of the IVC from the adrenal gland. New adhesions are clipped and harmonized. The posterior lateral area of the adrenal is dissected, targeting the apex to control the adrenal vein draining into the IVC. An inadvertent bleed occurs, which is managed with surgical cell coverage. The superior pole of the kidney is then separated from the margin of the residual adrenal gland.

The surgeon releases the margin of the adrenal attached to the inferior surface of the liver to manage severe adhesions and inadvertent bleeding at the junction of the adrenal vein with the IVC. The adrenal vein is clipped between two wet clips, and the superior margin of the adrenal attached to the liver surface is dissected and harmonized. Neonet Fibrillar is placed beneath the raw liver surface for added security, and the adrenal gland is bagged and retrieved, completing the adrenalectomy.

Over a span of two years and nine months, a total of forty-two patients have undergone adrenalectomy, of which thirty-one have been laparoscopic. Mean blood loss has been two hundred and twenty-eight mils and mean post-operative stay has been three point four days. With increasing experience, redo urology surgeries may also be offered minimally invasive benefits in carefully selected settings. However, the threshold for conversion to open should be low, with a standing consent for same. The experience of laparoscopy places the urologists at the forefront to venture into such demanding cases and provide minimally invasive benefits to a larger indication of patient subset. Thank you.
